sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, ACTION7.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 43, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Character (black L-shape with white dots) starts at (15,18)-(23,26), moves 3 cells per action - Dark gray L-shape at (15,36)-(23,44), moves opposite horizontally, same direction vertically - Yellow L-shape target at (45,51)-(53,59) - likely the goal - Sky blue divider at cols 30-32 - character can pass through - Yellow bar at col 63 shrinks by 1 per action (HUD/timer) - Movement: RIGHT moves char right 3, dark left 3; DOWN moves both down 3
- Plan: Navigate character to overlap the yellow L-shape. Do 11 RIGHTs first (to avoid collision with dark object), then 10 DOWNs.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 42 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
From the history, I can see the character's dot pattern never changes orientation - it always has the same L-shape pattern:
- 3 dots in a horizontal row (top of character)
- 3 dots in a vertical column (right side of character)
- Corner at top-right
Looking at steps 0-29, the character always has the same orientation. ACTION7 doesn't rotate the character at all - it just moves it UP by 3.
Looking at step 29: character at (45,51)-(53,59) with dots Y,Y,Y,W,W - 3 yellow dots absorbed from the top horizontal bar of the yellow shape.
The yellow shape has a vertical bar on the LEFT that the character can't absorb because the character's vertical bar is on the RIGHT.
So the key question is: how do I absorb the left vertical bar of the yellow shape?
Looking at the yellow shape at (45,51)-(53,56), it's an L-shape with:
- Horizontal bar at top (row 45, cols 51-56)
- Vertical bar on left (rows 45-53, col 51)
The character's L-shape has:
- Horizontal bar at top (row 46, cols 52,55,58)
- Vertical bar on right (rows 46,49,52 at col 58)
Wait, let me re-examine. The character's dots are at positions relative to the character's bounding box. The character is 8x9 (rows 45-53, cols 51-59). The dots are at:
- (46,52), (46,55), (46,58) - horizontal row
- (46,58), (49,58), (52,58) - vertical column
Corner at (46,58) = top-right of the character.
The yellow shape occupies (45,51)-(53,56). Let me think about its structure:
- It's an L-shape with 36 pixels
- Horizontal bar: row 45, cols 51-56 (6 cells, but it's 2 cells thick, so 12 pixels)
- Vertical bar: rows 45-53, col 51 (9 cells, but 2 cells thick, so 18 pixels)
- But there's overlap at the corner, so total = 12 + 18 - 4 = 26... that doesn't match 36.
Actually, let me reconsider. The yellow shape has 36 pixels and bbox (45,51)-(53,56), which is 9 rows × 6 cols = 54 cells max. 36 pixels means it's a 2-pixel thick L-shape.
The character absorbs yellow pixels when it overlaps them. Currently the character absorbs the top horizontal bar (3 yellow dots) but not the left vertical bar.
Maybe the solution is not about rotating the character but about matching the yellow shape's orientation. What if I need to move the character so that its L-shape exactly overlaps the yellow L-shape?
But the character's L-shape is corner-at-top-right while the yellow shape is corner-at-top-left. They're mirror images!
Wait, maybe the goal isn't to absorb all yellow pixels. Maybe the goal is something else entirely. Let me reconsider what "completing" the level looks like.
Looking at the history, at step 29 the character absorbed 3 yellow dots. But the level didn't complete. At step 30 (SPACE), the character stayed in place and dots remained yellow. At step 31 (ACTION7), the character moved up and dots reverted.
What if the level completes when ALL yellow dots on the character are yellow? Currently only 1 out of 5 dots is yellow. The character can absorb 3 yellow dots when overlapping the top of the yellow shape.
But the character's vertical bar is on the right, and the yellow shape's vertical bar is on the left. They don't overlap in that region.
What if I need to move the character LEFT so that its vertical bar overlaps the yellow shape's vertical bar? But the yellow shape's vertical bar is at col 51-52, and the character's vertical bar is at col 58. If I move the character left by 6 columns (2 RIGHT movements in reverse = 2 LEFT), the character's vertical bar would be at col 52, overlapping the yellow shape's vertical bar.
Wait, but the character moves 3 cells per action. So 2 LEFT actions would move it 6 cells left.
Current character: (42,51)-(50,59)
After 1 LEFT: (42,48)-(50,56)
After 2 LEFT: (42,45)-(50,53)
But the yellow shape is at (45,51)-(53,56). After 2 LEFT, the character would be at cols 45-53, which doesn't overlap the yellow shape at cols 51-56 except for cols 51-53.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me think differently.
What if the goal is to make the character's dots ALL yellow? The character has 5 dots. When overlapping the yellow shape, some dots turn yellow. Currently only 1-3 dots turn yellow depending on position.
What if I need to position the character so that ALL 5 dots overlap yellow pixels? That would require the character's L-shape to perfectly overlap the yellow L-shape.
But the character's L-shape is corner-at-top-right and the yellow L-shape is corner-at-top-left. They're mirror images and can't perfectly overlap.
Unless... the character CAN be rotated. Let me look more carefully at the ACTION7 behavior.
From the history:
- Step 31: ACTION7, char moved from (45,51) to (42,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 32: ACTION7, char moved from (42,51) to (39,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 33: ACTION7, char moved from (39,51) to (36,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 34: ACTION7, char moved from (36,51) to (33,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 35: ACTION7, char moved from (33,51) to (30,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 36: ACTION7, char moved from (30,51) to (27,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 37: ACTION7, char moved from (27,51) to (24,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 38: DOWN, char moved from (24,51) to (27,51) - moved DOWN 3
- Step 39: ACTION7, char moved from (27,51) to (24,51) - moved UP 3 (but was flagged no_impact)
- Step 40: ACTION7, char moved from (45,51) to (42,51) - moved UP 3
- Step 42: DOWN, char moved from (42,51) to (45,51) then ACTION7 moved to (42,51)
Wait, I'm confused about the step numbers. Let me just look at the most recent data.
Current state: character at (42,51)-(50,59) with 1 yellow dot.
The yellow shape is at (45,51)-(53,56) with 36 pixels.
I think I've been overcomplicating this. Let me reconsider the problem.
Maybe the goal is simply to have the character overlap the yellow shape such that all the character's dots are yellow. Since the character can only absorb 3 dots from the top horizontal bar, maybe I need to find a way to also absorb dots from the vertical bar.
But the character's vertical bar is on the right (col 58) and the yellow shape's vertical bar is on the left (col 51). They're on opposite sides.
What if I move the character LEFT so its right-side vertical bar overlaps the yellow shape's left-side vertical bar? But that would require the character to be positioned such that col 58 of the character aligns with col 51-52 of the yellow shape. That means moving the character LEFT by about 6 columns.
Actually wait, let me re-examine the yellow shape more carefully. The yellow shape bbox is (45,51)-(53,56). That means it spans rows 45-53 and cols 51-56. The character at (45,51)-(53,59) spans rows 45-53 and cols 51-59.
When the character overlaps the yellow shape:
- Character's horizontal bar at row 46, cols 52, 55, 58 overlaps yellow at row 45 (not quite - row 46 vs row 45)
- Actually the character's dots are at row 46, but the yellow shape starts at row 45. So the overlap might be at rows 46-47.
Hmm, I'm getting confused by the exact pixel positions. Let me just try moving the character to different positions relative to the yellow shape and see what gets absorbed.
Actually, I just realized something. Looking at the history output that was truncated, I see that after step 27 (DOWN), the character was at (39,51)-(47,59) and had a yellow dot at (46,58). This means the character started absorbing yellow pixels when it reached row 46 (overlapping the yellow shape at row 45+).
At step 29, the character was at (45,51)-(53,59) and had 3 yellow dots at (46,52), (46,55), (46,58). These are all on the horizontal bar of the character.
The character's vertical bar dots are at (46,58), (49,58), (52,58). The yellow shape is at cols 51-56, so col 58 is outside the yellow shape. That's why the vertical bar dots never turn yellow.
To get the vertical bar dots to turn yellow, I need to move the character LEFT so that col 58 moves into the yellow shape's column range (51-56).
If I move LEFT by 3 (1 action), the character moves from cols 51-59 to cols 48-56. The vertical bar would be at col 55, which is within the yellow shape's range. But the horizontal bar would be at cols 49, 52, 55 - cols 49 and 52 might be outside the yellow shape.
Let me try: move LEFT once, then DOWN to overlap the yellow shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try a different approach: move LEFT so character's vertical bar overlaps yellow shape's vertical bar
# Current char at (42,51)-(50,59), need to go to yellow shape at (45,51)-(53,56)
# First move LEFT to shift character's vertical bar into yellow shape's columns
# Then DOWN to overlap vertically
action(['LEFT', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
